Lancashire Standards History

Straight comparison of the published short-course consideration times from 2016 to 2027. Times are displayed exactly as published. 2021 is shown as a deliberate gap because no equivalent championship standard was identified.
How to read this: a lower time is a harder standard. The comparison ignores qualification windows and simply asks whether the published standard for the same category, age and event became faster, slower or stayed the same. No 2021 standard is shown because no equivalent championship table has been identified.

Meet entry planner Beta

Builds a balanced meet programme from the same development priorities used in Athlete Explorer. It does not predict performances.

Experimental guidance only

This page is a beta planning aid. It is designed to support conversations between athletes, parents and coaches, not to replace coach advice or individual judgement. It cannot assess technical readiness, training performance, injury, confidence, race strategy or the demands of a particular meet.

Coach advice always takes priority. Young athletes should not specialise in one stroke too early. The stroke that feels strongest now may change as the athlete grows, matures and develops. Building skill and confidence across all four strokes and a sensible range of distances creates better long-term swimmers.

Planning rule
The entry limit rebuilds a balanced six, eight or ten-event programme. Established competitors normally receive the full selected six, eight or ten-event programme; confidence ranks the choices rather than removing sensible options. Foundation and developing athletes retain broad stroke coverage and suitable 100m progression before demanding 200m events. Advanced athletes receive more targeted county opportunities, qualification defence and pathway events. Distance-event workload is controlled, and 800m and 1500m Freestyle are never recommended together. Coaches retain final judgement.
Enter a time to compare.

How to use the Lancashire Pathway Planner

I created this dashboard to help our athletes, parents and coaches understand progress, make better-informed race choices and have clearer conversations. It is not intended to turn every session or competition into a chase for a qualifying time. The aim is to support long-term development, good technique, broad racing experience and confident decision-making.

Technique is king. Listen carefully to your coaches, apply the detail they give you and give your absolute best in every session. Qualification comes from consistent, purposeful work over time; progress is rarely linear.

DashboardAthlete ExplorerClub AnalysisLancashire TimesMeet PlannerTime CheckerData & privacy

Dashboard

The dashboard gives a quick club-wide view of qualification and near-qualification.

Qualified athletes
Unique athletes with at least one valid in-window performance at or faster than the applicable 2027 consideration time.
Confirmed qualifiers
Athlete-event combinations, so one athlete may contribute several qualifying events.
Within threshold
Recorded athlete-event combinations outside the standard but within the selected percentage slider.
Closest confirmed targets
Actual recorded swims, ordered by percentage gap to the standard. These are not predictions.
Qualification funnel
Shows how the club moves from recorded events towards closer targets and confirmed qualifications.
Qualification change log
Used after a future data refresh to show newly qualified events, improved event PBs and newly recorded events. The current published version is the baseline.

Athlete Explorer

Select an athlete to review every championship event against the correct age category at 31 December 2027.

Best in qualification window
The fastest converted short-course performance achieved from 9 February 2026 to 13 January 2027. This is the only performance used for qualification.
Best outside window
The fastest historical result outside the qualification window. It provides context but cannot establish 2027 qualification.
Gap to standard
The difference between the athlete's valid best and the 2027 consideration time. Percentage gaps allow fairer comparison between events of different lengths.
PB progression
Compares the fastest pre-window PB with the fastest eligible in-window result. Dates are shown so the comparison can be checked.
Window progression
Compares the first eligible swim in the window with the best eligible swim. A slower recent race does not remove an earlier PB.
Current form
Uses the latest three eligible swims only. Labels are cautious: improving, plateauing near PB, broadly stable, variable form or limited evidence.
Performance timeline
Plots all matching historical swims. Lower is faster. Use stroke, distance, period and course controls to explore progression.
Performance evidence
The underlying race history, including date, event, source time, converted SC time, meet and whether the swim falls inside the qualification window.

Current priorities Beta

This experimental feature ranks evidence-based guidance rather than replacing coach judgement.

Qualification security
Qualifications within 0.5% are marginal; 0.5–1.5% inside are worth protecting; more than 1.5% inside are treated as secure.
New opportunities
Closer Lancashire opportunities outrank ordinary development suggestions, with the acceptable gap narrowing as the qualification deadline approaches.
Stroke breadth
A genuinely missing or materially under-represented stroke is prioritised above an ordinary untested progression. A heavily raced stroke is never recommended merely to add more races.
Progression ladder
50m evidence supports 100m development; multiple competitive 100m performances support 200m development. Missing results mean unknown, not unsuitable.
Distance freestyle
400m Freestyle requires a 200m time at or within 5% of county standard and an age-appropriate profile. 800m requires established 400m evidence; 1500m requires established 800m evidence.
200m IM
Recommended as a staple development event only when age-appropriate evidence exists across several strokes and distances.
Confidence
Race familiarity, current form and proximity to the standard influence which recommendations appear.

Club Analysis

This page looks beyond a filtered results list and shows where the club has strength, depth and gaps.

Qualification heat maps
Unique qualified athletes by stroke, distance and 2027 age group. A number represents athletes, not qualifying events.
Event depth
For each event, separates unique qualified athletes, athletes within the selected threshold and athletes with a recorded valid time.
Next qualification targets
Actual recorded performances closest to the standard, ranked by percentage gap.
Qualification and target depth
Summarises unique athletes by stroke while showing event totals separately, avoiding claims such as “six athletes qualified” when two athletes qualified across six events.
Age-group pipeline
Shows the number of athletes with recorded evidence, close targets and qualifications in each age group.
Participation coverage
Shows how widely each stroke is being raced, independent of qualification.
Event coverage gaps
Counts athletes without a valid in-window performance in events up to 200m. A gap is a prompt for coaching judgement, not an instruction to enter every athlete.
Detailed results
The underlying athlete-event records used by the analysis and filters.

2027 Lancashire Times Analysis

This section compares the published 2026 and 2027 consideration times.

Faster / harder
The 2027 standard is faster than the 2026 standard. Red indicates that qualification has become more demanding.
Slower / easier
The 2027 standard is slower. Green indicates that the standard has relaxed.
Unchanged
The standard is identical.
Change in seconds and percentage
Seconds show the absolute movement; percentage change gives better context across different race lengths.
Detailed table, matrix and grouped summary
Different ways to inspect the same standards data. Filters should always apply to the figures and rows shown.

A harder standard does not mean an athlete has gone backwards. It means the benchmark has changed.

Shared Development Engine — Beta

Athlete Explorer and Meet Planner use the same underlying development model. Athlete Explorer explains the athlete’s priorities; Meet Planner translates those priorities into a balanced six, eight or ten-event programme.

Protect
Qualifications that are vulnerable enough to remain visible in the racing programme.
Build
Stroke breadth, missing race foundations and areas that need more balanced development.
Progress
The strongest evidence-supported move from 50m to 100m, from 100m to 200m, or into an appropriate IM pathway.
Long-term
The broader direction the athlete is growing towards, such as IM, middle-distance or advanced championship racing.
Why now
The coaching reason the priority is relevant at this point in the athlete’s development.
Evidence
Exploratory, Developing, Established or Strong describes the depth of the supporting race evidence, not whether the recommendation is good or bad.
Next step
A practical action for a future meet or development phase. Numerical recommendation scores remain internal and are used only to rank competing options.
Distinct progression
Progress must identify a different event from Improve. If no separate event is ready, the card explains that more progression evidence is needed rather than repeating the same race.

Meet Planner — Beta

Meet Planner builds a balanced competition programme rather than simply listing the athlete’s closest qualifying times. It is planning guidance only; the final programme should be agreed collaboratively with the athlete’s coach.

Foundation athletes

Less-developed athletes normally establish all four 50m events and build suitable 100m races before moving into demanding 200m events. Stroke breadth is deliberately prioritised because early strengths can change significantly as athletes grow.

Developing all-rounders

Once broad 50m and 100m evidence exists, the planner balances realistic Lancashire opportunities, missing stroke development, suitable 200m events and the IM pathway. A credible 100m IM is strong evidence for considering 200m IM.

Advanced athletes

More advanced athletes may focus on two or three principal strokes, county opportunities and vulnerable qualifications. Secondary strokes still need occasional maintenance, but a recently raced 50m may be omitted for one suitable meet. Near the qualification deadline, a realistic county-time chase may temporarily take priority.

Six, eight and ten entries

Each entry limit creates a newly balanced programme rather than simply cutting down a longer ranked list. Established competitors should normally receive the full six, eight or ten options. Confidence changes the order and strength of the recommendations rather than causing sensible established events to disappear. Foundation athletes may still receive fewer where there is genuinely too little evidence to support more. Larger programmes expand breadth and progression without filling every remaining place with long-distance events.

IM and distance progression

The 200m IM requires both stroke readiness and enough evidence to sustain eight lengths. An existing 200m IM automatically confirms readiness, particularly when it is within 10% of the county standard. For an athlete who has not yet raced the 200m IM, the planner first requires recorded race evidence across all four 50m strokes, with at least three broadly competent and no more than one manageable weakness. It then looks for distance evidence from an established 200m event, regular racing across multiple 100m events, a credible 100m IM, or strong age-appropriate performances close to county standard. It is held back when two or more strokes remain materially underdeveloped or when the athlete has only sprint evidence without sufficient depth. The 400m IM remains an advanced option requiring a strong 200m IM and broader 200m race pace.

Competition schedules, session clashes, technical readiness, injury, confidence and individual circumstances remain outside the model. Coaches retain final judgement.

Time Checker

Enter a category, age, event and time to compare it directly with the 2027 standard.

Improvement needed
The exact time and percentage required to reach the standard.
Monthly and weekly guide
A simple division of the required improvement across the remaining time. It is a planning prompt only; swimming improvement is not linear.
Club context
An approximate position among recorded Fleetwood Mako's athletes in the same category and event. It is not a Lancashire ranking.
Practical target plan
Training and race-skill prompts to discuss with the athlete's coach.

Technique is king. Listen to your coaches and give your absolute best every session. A Lancashire consideration time requires at least four purposeful sessions per week. Development Squad athletes should attend all core sessions: Monday, Wednesday, Friday and the invite-only Saturday morning session. Four sessions is the absolute minimum for Performance Squad athletes, and Saturday morning technique and skills is strongly encouraged.

Race-pace efficiency, planned breathing patterns, starts, turns, underwater speed, nutrition, recovery, stretching and appropriate strength and conditioning all matter. Athletes must own the process: keep a swim log, record splits and stroke counts, ask for feedback and set SMART goals with their coach.

Data, qualification rules and privacy

Qualification window
9 February 2026 to 13 January 2027.
Age
The athlete's age at 31 December 2027 determines the applicable standard.
Course handling
Long-course swims use the converted short-course time supplied in the SportsEngine report.
Missing event
No valid performance is listed for that athlete and event within the qualification window.
Historical swims
Used for context, progression and charts only. They never establish qualification for 2027.
Privacy
Exact dates of birth are used only during preparation to calculate the correct age category. They are not displayed or stored in the shared app. Only age at 31 December 2027 is shown.

All recommendations are aids to conversation. Technical readiness, health, confidence, meet schedules and individual development can only be judged properly by the coaching team.

Why this tool exists

This dashboard was created to give Fleetwood Mako's athletes, parents and coaches a clearer understanding of Lancashire qualification, progression and event planning. It should encourage evidence-based decisions without losing sight of what matters most: excellent technique, consistent training, broad skill development, enjoyment and long-term progress.

Use the information to ask better questions, celebrate progress and agree the next sensible step with your coach.

Data, future updates and methodology

This version embeds the full SportsEngine snapshot, including historical performances. Qualification uses only swims dated 9 February 2026 to 13 January 2027 and the converted short-course time supplied by SportsEngine.

Privacy
Exact dates of birth are not stored or displayed in this shared app. Only each athlete’s 2027 championship age category is retained.

Future updates

Athlete data is maintained centrally by Fleetwood Mako's and refreshed through controlled version updates. The public planner is read-only, so users cannot replace, export or alter the embedded dataset.

Updated SportsEngine extracts are checked and incorporated into a new published release.

Decision rules

  • Qualification: fastest valid in-window converted SC time.
  • Age: championship category at 31 December 2027.
  • Historical results: used for progression only; never counted as 2027 qualification.
  • Meet planner: actual results and missing event coverage only; no speculative time prediction.
  • Long-distance suggestions: restricted to athletes with supporting evidence.

Snapshot contains . Missing events mean no valid result is listed for that event in the qualification window.

Fleetwood Mako’s • Internal planning and athlete guidance tool • Qualification status uses only eligible performances achieved within the published window.
Lancashire Pathway Planner — V3.0 Development 58
2027 Lancashire Championships · Race data current to 15 July 2026
© Fleetwood Mako's Aquatics Club
Technique is king. Listen to your coaches. Give your absolute best every session. Own your process.
💬Feedback

Before you begin

The Lancashire Pathway Planner has been created to help Fleetwood Mako's athletes, coaches and families understand progress towards the Lancashire Championships, set sensible goals and have better development conversations.

This planner is not a team-selection or athlete-comparison tool.

A faster published time does not automatically make one athlete the correct selection ahead of another. Team and relay selection is a coaching decision based on the needs of the whole team and the specific competition.

Selection involves much more than times

  • Current form, readiness and ability to perform under pressure.
  • Legal, technically reliable racing and the likelihood of avoiding disqualification.
  • Starts, turns, finishes, underwater skills and execution of race plans.
  • Relay takeovers, relay reliability and suitability for a particular relay combination.
  • Training commitment, attendance, coachability and willingness to apply feedback.
  • Attitude, teamwork, conduct, availability and contribution to the wider team.
  • Event schedules, recovery demands, team balance and tactical considerations.

Use the information respectfully

Use this planner to compare your own progress with the Lancashire standards, not to judge, rank, criticise or target another athlete.

Every athlete develops differently. Celebrate teammates' progress, be decent to one another and discuss questions about development, competitions or selection directly and respectfully with the coaching team.

Where the base data comes from

The underlying licensed competition results and Swim England membership identifiers are publicly available through Swim England and Swimming Results. The planner brings that information together and adds Fleetwood Mako's analysis and guidance.

Coach advice takes priority

The planner cannot assess everything a coach considers, including technical readiness, confidence, health, training performance, race-day circumstances or the needs of a team. Its outputs are guidance only.

Welcome to Meet Planner Beta

Meet Planner is designed to help athletes, parents and coaches build balanced competition programmes. It looks beyond the nearest qualifying time and also considers development stage, stroke breadth, race experience and sensible event progression.

Foundation before specialisation

Less-developed athletes usually need broad 50m coverage and suitable 100m progression before moving into more demanding 200m events.

Depth before 200m IM

Broad 50m ability is not enough on its own. The planner also looks for evidence that the athlete can sustain eight lengths through 100m depth, an established 200m event, a credible 100m IM or strong age-appropriate race standards.

Clear recommendation language

Priorities explain why an event matters, how much supporting race evidence exists and the practical next step. Evidence labels describe the depth of information available, not whether a recommendation is good or bad.

Stroke breadth

The planner may recommend a weaker or less-raced stroke to support long-term development, rather than simply repeating favourite events.

Balanced race load

Six, eight and ten-event weekends need different programme shapes. The planner expands the programme carefully, balancing qualification targets, stroke breadth, progression and the physical load of longer races.

Workload matters

Demanding distance events need careful spacing. For example, 800m and 1500m Freestyle should not normally be selected in the same weekend.

Competition schedules, session clashes, technical readiness and individual circumstances remain outside the model. Recommendations are designed to support informed discussion, not replace coaching judgement.
